text.title: model-evaluation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for model-evaluation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Select 2–3 primary metrics per task type and define success thresholds before running any evaluation.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection is p-hacking. Thresholds defined after seeing results are uninformative.

rule.statement: Use multiple judge models (GPT-4 + Claude) and average scores to reduce evaluator bias; report confidence intervals, not single-point estimates.
rule.rationale: GPT-4 prefers GPT-4 outputs; Claude prefers Claude outputs. Multi-judge averaging partially cancels bias. Single-point estimates hide variance that matters for production decisions.

rule.statement: Build your evaluation dataset from real production queries, not synthetic ones; minimum 200 samples; version it alongside model/prompt versions.
rule.rationale: Synthetic datasets miss the long tail of real user behavior. Mismatched dataset versions make cross-evaluation comparisons meaningless.

rule.statement: Define success metrics and calculate required sample size before traffic splitting; run until p < 0.05 and check for novelty effects.
rule.rationale: Underpowered A/B tests produce false positives that lead to wrong model selection decisions.

rule.statement: Sample 1–10% of production traffic for quality evaluation; run LLM-as-judge on 0.1–1%; set cost alerts at 2x baseline and page at 5x.
rule.rationale: Full evaluation on 100% of production traffic is cost-prohibitive; sampling provides sufficient signal. Cost alerts catch runaway agent loops before they drain budget.

rule.statement: Block deployment if quality drops >5% on core metrics versus the production baseline.
rule.rationale: Catching quality regressions in CI prevents silent degradation in production.

rule.statement: Evaluation agents must rate-limit concurrent LLM calls and must not cache LLM-as-judge results across prompt versions.
rule.rationale: Parallel evaluation of 1000 samples at 50 concurrent calls hits API rate limits. Stale evaluations from a prior prompt version corrupt cross-version comparisons.
